output_text:
Summary: Critique of the four-paragraph abstract, focused on internal inconsistencies, under-specified methods, and a structural limitation in the experimental design.

## Substantive issues

1. **Blinding contradicts the rationale analysis.** Para 2 says "system identities are stripped from both inputs and rationales before scoring, so the judge has no signal as to which system authored either candidate." Para 3 then reports that GPT-4o "explicitly names 'Whisper-style fluency'" in 8.4% of its rationales. If "Whisper-style fluency" survived stripping, the stripping wasn't of identifying tokens but of something narrower; if it didn't survive, you couldn't have measured it. Reword to distinguish (a) blinding the judge at inference time from (b) preserving the raw rationale for post-hoc regression.

2. **Self-preference is testable for only one of three judges.** The design pairs three judges (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Gemini-1.5-Pro) against four ASR systems, of which only one (Whisper) is plausibly a "family member" of any judge (GPT-4o ↔ OpenAI). Claude and Gemini have no in-family ASR system in the suite, so by construction they cannot exhibit the bias being measured. The asymmetric finding ("GPT-4o yes, others no") is therefore weak evidence of self-preference — it could equally be GPT-4o preferring fluent disfluent-suppressing transcripts in general. Either add an Anthropic/Google-trained ASR (e.g., Universal-1, USM-derived) or recast the contribution as "in-family bias measurable for one OpenAI pair," not as a general self-preference benchmark.

3. **"ASR sibling" is an assertion, not a demonstration.** Shared corporate origin doesn't establish a training-distribution link between GPT-4o and Whisper. Either cite evidence that GPT-4o's text training included Whisper-style transcripts, or drop the "sibling" framing and describe the effect mechanistically (e.g., "transcripts with Whisper's characteristic hallucination/fluency profile").

4. **Judgment count doesn't reconcile with the design.** 4 systems × C(4,2)=6 pairs × 4,000 items × 3 judges = 72,000 judgments. You report 36,000 — exactly half. State the sampling scheme (3 of 6 pairs per item? Half the items per judge?) in the abstract; readers will notice.

5. **"Even when objective WER is held constant" is underspecified.** Did you stratify pairs by WER bin, control with a covariate in a regression on preferences, or restrict to pairs where ΔWER ≈ 0? The mechanism matters: the 11.3% figure changes a lot depending on which.

6. **Bradley-Terry strengths vs. win-rate percentages.** Para 3 says preferences are aggregated into BT strengths, then reports "11.3% higher win rates." Pick one summary or explain the mapping. Also clarify whether 11.3% is absolute (e.g., 55.7% vs. 44.4%) or relative.

7. **JudgeFix evaluation risks circularity.** The calibration constant is "learned per-judge," and the residual bias is reported "across our test split." Make explicit: was the constant fit on a disjoint train split, and is the 1.9% measured only on held-out audio/items/judge-pair combinations? Otherwise 1.9% is a fit residual, not a generalization claim. Also report the pre-intervention Cohen's κ — 0.81 post-fix is uninterpretable without the baseline.

8. **"First standardized benchmark"** invites adversarial reviews. Soften to "a standardized benchmark" unless you've done a thorough prior-work scan and can cite the absence in the related-work section.

9. **Three judges is thin for benchmark claims.** Acknowledge in the abstract that the leaderboard infrastructure is designed for extension; otherwise the claim of a benchmark (vs. a study) is hard to defend.

10. **Minor wording.** "4,000 paired audio-transcript items" is ambiguous — each item is one audio + four transcripts, not a pair. "Drop-in wrapper for major LLM-judge libraries" should name them (lm-eval-harness? AlpacaEval? PandaLM?) or be cut.

## Suggested priorities

Most urgent before submission: fix issues 1, 2, and 7 — they're substantive enough that a careful reviewer will reject on any one of them. Issues 4, 5, and 6 are easy precision fixes. The rest are polish.

Uncertainty disclosure: I'm assuming standard pairwise-benchmark conventions for the judgment-count arithmetic and BT/win-rate distinction; if your protocol differs (e.g., single random pair per item per judge), points 4 and 6 may not apply as stated. I haven't verified whether GPT-4o's training corpus is publicly known to include Whisper outputs — point 3 depends on that.
